notes: Two-part paper. Part A: 22 fill-in-the-blank questions, 30 marks, 1 hr. Part B: 5 long-answer questions, 90 marks, 2 hrs. Course code AAOC C321 (older code, same course). Date 08-12-2007.

questions.stem: Lumped parameters are characterized by _______ (differential eqs. / partial differential eqs.)
common_errors: Confusing lumped with distributed parameter systems
questions.solution_method: Lumped parameter systems are described by ordinary differential equations; distributed parameter systems require partial differential equations.

questions.stem: System described by Y = d²x/dt² + dx/dt + 7√x, where X is input and Y is output, is a _______ system (Non linear/linear and Time variant/invariant).
common_errors: Missing that √x makes the system nonlinear
Wrongly calling it time-variant
questions.solution_method: The term 7√x is nonlinear (not expressible as linear combination of input/output). Coefficients are constant so time-invariant. Answer: nonlinear time-invariant.

questions.stem: In an ideal position control servo mechanism, back emf constant is numerically equal to _______ constant.
common_errors: Confusing torque constant with back-emf constant
questions.solution_method: In SI units for an ideal DC motor, back emf constant Kb equals torque constant Kt numerically.

questions.stem: For a unity negative feedback system, forward path gain is K/(s+9). Sensitivity of the system, in case of open loop and closed loop to small changes in K (K=0.4) at ω=1 rad/s is _______ and _______ respectively.
common_errors: Forgetting open-loop sensitivity is always 1
Incorrect denominator in closed-loop sensitivity formula
questions.solution_method: Open-loop sensitivity S^T_K = 1. Closed-loop sensitivity S^T_K = 1/(1+GH). With G=K/(s+9), H=1, K=0.4, s=j1: denominator = 1 + 0.4/(j1+9) = (9.4+j1)/(9+j1). Compute magnitude.

questions.stem: In Q.4, if required time constant for closed loop system is 10 ms then the value of K and corresponding steady state gain is _______ and _______ respectively.
common_errors: Confusing open-loop and closed-loop time constant
Not computing DC gain of closed-loop TF
questions.solution_method: CL TF = K/(s+9+K). Time constant τ = 1/(9+K) = 0.01 s → K = 91. DC gain = K/(9+K) = 91/100.

questions.stem: If a first order system works in open loop mode, its steady state gain and the speed of response is _______ and _______ respectively, as compared to closed loop mode.
common_errors: Reversing which mode is faster
questions.solution_method: Open loop has higher steady-state gain but slower speed of response compared to closed loop (feedback reduces gain and speeds up response by increasing bandwidth).

questions.stem: A 6-stack stepper motor has _____ numbers of teeth if the angular displacement between stacks of stator teeth is 4° (assuming stack rotor teeth aligns with its stator).
common_errors: Using wrong formula relating stacks and tooth pitch
questions.solution_method: For a stepper motor, step angle = 360°/(N_r × N_s) where N_r = rotor teeth, N_s = stacks. The angular displacement between stacks = 360°/(N_s × N_r). With N_s=6 and displacement=4°: N_r = 360/(6×4) = 15 teeth.

questions.stem: In Synchro transmitter, at some position of its rotor the voltage in one coil is maximum while across other two is zero; this position is known as _______ and the same name is given to the control transformer rotor position if the rotors of synchro pair are at _______.
common_errors: Confusing electrical zero with null position
questions.solution_method: The position is called 'electrical zero'. The control transformer rotor is at electrical zero when the synchro pair rotors are at 90° to each other (null output condition).

questions.stem: The Hydraulic actuator will work as an ideal integrator if leakage and _______ flow are negligible. (compressible/turbulent)
questions.solution_method: Compressible flow must be negligible (incompressible fluid assumption) for the hydraulic actuator to behave as an ideal integrator.

questions.stem: For the same horse power, hydraulic actuators are _______ than electrical motors. (lighter/heavier)
questions.solution_method: Hydraulic actuators have higher power-to-weight ratio and are lighter than equivalent electrical motors for the same horsepower.

questions.stem: Out of Pneumatic and hydraulic systems, which one has shorter response time? _______
questions.solution_method: Hydraulic systems have shorter response time due to the incompressibility of the fluid (faster force transmission) compared to pneumatic systems.

questions.stem: The _______ damped step response of a second order system oscillates with constant frequency and magnitude.
common_errors: Confusing underdamped with undamped
questions.solution_method: An undamped (ζ=0) second order system produces sustained oscillations at constant frequency and magnitude. Underdamped decays; overdamped/critically damped don't oscillate.

questions.stem: The response of a system for step input of 4 units is (1-e^{-4t})t u(t). If this system is excited by an input of e^{-5t} u(t), the steady state value of the response is _______.
common_errors: Not identifying TF from step response correctly
Misapplying final value theorem to non-step input
questions.solution_method: Step response = (1-e^{-4t})u(t)/4 implies H(s) = step_response_LT × s / (1/s) → deduce H(s). For input e^{-5t}u(t), Laplace = 1/(s+5). Output Y(s) = H(s)/(s+5). Apply FVT: lim_{s→0} s·Y(s). Since s=0 is not a pole issue here, evaluate at s=0.

questions.stem: The addition of only a zero in the closed loop transfer function results in _______ rise time and _______ peak overshoot.
common_errors: Confusing effect of zero addition vs pole addition
questions.solution_method: Adding a zero to the CLTF speeds up the response (decreases rise time) and increases peak overshoot.

questions.stem: For the system shown in Fig Q.15 (unity feedback with forward path 10/[s(5s+1)] and feedback path 0.5/[s(0.25s+1)]), the position error coefficient is _______ and acceleration error coefficient is _______.
common_errors: Treating non-unity feedback as unity feedback
Incorrectly computing equivalent open-loop TF
questions.solution_method: Reduce to equivalent unity feedback: G_eq = G/(1+GH-G). Compute Kp = lim_{s→0} G_eq and Ka = lim_{s→0} s²G_eq.

questions.stem: The open loop transfer function of a negative feedback system is K/[(s+1)(s+3)]. The range of K for which system exhibits the overdamped response is _______.
common_errors: Not finding the critical K where roots become complex
questions.solution_method: CL CE: s²+4s+3+K=0. Roots real (overdamped) when discriminant ≥ 0: 16-4(3+K) ≥ 0 → K ≤ 1. Also K > 0 for unity negative feedback. So 0 < K ≤ 1.

questions.stem: The characteristic equation of a negative feedback system is s³ + 4s² + 5s + K = 0. The range of K for system to be stable is _______.
common_errors: Forgetting K > 0 condition
questions.solution_method: Routh array: s³: 1,5; s²: 4,K; s¹: (20-K)/4; s⁰: K. For stability: (20-K)/4 > 0 → K < 20, and K > 0. Range: 0 < K < 20.

questions.stem: For a system to be stable, the gain at phase cross-over frequency should be less than _______ db.
questions.solution_method: At phase cross-over frequency (where phase = -180°), |G(jω)| < 1, i.e., gain < 0 dB for stability.

questions.stem: The transfer function of a compensation network is (s+5)/(s+0.5). This represents a _______ network. (lead/lag)
common_errors: Confusing zero location vs pole location for lead/lag
questions.solution_method: Zero at s=-5, pole at s=-0.5. Zero magnitude > pole magnitude (|zero| > |pole|), so this is a lead compensator (zero closer to origin than pole → lag; farther → lead).

questions.stem: The maximum phase lead required from a lead network is 30°. The value of α (or a) is _______.
common_errors: Using wrong formula direction (solving for α vs φ_max)

questions.stem: The frequency plot of a system is given in Fig Q.21 (polar plot showing gain=-0.4 at -1 crossing and phase=-110° at unit circle crossing). The gain margin is _______ db and phase margin is _______.
common_errors: Reading GM from wrong point
Sign error on GM in dB
questions.solution_method: GM = -20log(magnitude at phase=-180°) = -20log(0.4) ≈ 7.96 dB. PM = 180° - 110° = 70° (phase angle at unit circle crossing).

questions.stem: In a compensation network, the zero location is at -0.5 and at dc frequency the network provides an attenuation of 14 db. The location of compensatory pole is _______ and the frequency at which it provides maximum phase lead is _______ rad/s.
common_errors: Confusing attenuation sign convention
Wrong formula for frequency of max phase

questions.stem: Position of a camera is to be controlled via an AC servo motor through a gear train (N1/N2=1/2). Synchro error detector Ks=30 V/rad, amplifier gain KA=20 V/V, motor torque Tm=K1Vc-K2ωm (read from torque-speed curve: stall torque at 50V control=5 Nm, no-load speed=955 rpm). Load inertia JL=1 kg·m², friction BL=4 Nm·rad/s. Motor inertia and friction negligible. (i) Draw block diagram. (ii) Determine θL(s)/θR(s). (iii) Determine magnitude of step input for 5° final camera position.
common_errors: Incorrect gear ratio reflected inertia/friction
Wrong extraction of K1, K2 from torque-speed curve
Forgetting to include synchro and amplifier in loop
questions.solution_method: Extract K1=Tm_stall/Vc=5/50=0.1 Nm/V, K2=Tm_stall/ω_no-load=5/(955×2π/60). Reflect load through gear ratio N1/N2: J_eq=JL(N1/N2)²+Jm, B_eq=BL(N1/N2)²+Bm. Motor TF: θm(s)/Vc(s). Cascade with gear, synchro, amplifier. Use final value theorem for step input to get 5°.

questions.stem: Sketch the Nyquist plot for G(s) = K(1+0.5s)(1+s)/[(1+10s)(s-1)], choosing the appropriate Nyquist contour. Determine the range of K for which the closed loop system is stable.
common_errors: Wrong number of open-loop RHP poles (P=1 here)
Incorrect encirclement count
Not handling the RHP pole at s=1 in contour
questions.solution_method: G has one open-loop RHP pole (s=+1), so P=1. For CL stability, N=−P=−1 (one counter-clockwise encirclement of -1+j0). Sketch Nyquist: find real-axis crossings, determine encirclements as function of K. Solve for K range giving N=-1.

questions.stem: For the two-tank liquid level system shown: (a) write governing differential equations; (b) draw SFG and determine transfer function ΔQo(s)/ΔQd(s) using Mason's gain formula. Tank capacities are C1 and C2.
common_errors: Incorrect hydraulic resistance linearization
Missing interaction between tanks in SFG
Wrong application of Mason's formula (loop gains)
questions.solution_method: Linearize: C1·d(ΔH1)/dt = ΔQi - ΔH1/R1; C2·d(ΔH2)/dt = ΔH1/R1 - ΔQd - ΔH2/R2; ΔQo=ΔH2/R2. Take Laplace, construct SFG, apply Mason's: TF = (forward path gains)/(1 - sum of loop gains + ...).

questions.stem: For a unity negative feedback system with G(s) = K(s+15)/[s(s+2)]: (a) Draw neat root locus sketch showing all steps. (b) Given specs: max peak overshoot 4.32%, max settling time (2%) 800 ms, not overdamped — (i) determine range of K and show on RL; (ii) determine steady-state error for ramp input of 5 units (K=4).
common_errors: Incorrect breakaway/break-in points
Wrong mapping of Mp to ζ
Forgetting Kv for ramp SSE with type-1 system
questions.solution_method: RL: poles at 0,-2; zero at -15; 1 asymptote at 180°; breakaway/in computed via dK/ds=0. Specs: Mp=4.32% → ζ≥0.707; ts≤800ms → σ=ζωn≥5; not overdamped → ζ<1. Map onto RL to find K range. For SSE: ess=5/Kv, Kv=lim_{s→0} sG(s)|_{K=4} = 4×15/2 = 30, ess=5/30.

questions.stem: For the system with forward path K·4/[s(s²+2s+4)] in unity feedback: draw Bode magnitude (asymptotic) and phase plots on semi-log paper, then find: (a) value of K for system to be just unstable; (b) value of K if required gain margin is 6 dB, and for this K find phase margin and comment on stability.
common_errors: Wrong phase computation for complex poles
Incorrect gain crossover frequency
Not adjusting K before computing PM
questions.solution_method: Open loop G(s)=4K/[s(s²+2s+4)]. Find ω_pc where ∠G=-180°: factor s²+2s+4=(s+1)²+3, find phase. Compute |G(jω_pc)|; K_critical = 1/|G(jω_pc)|_{K=1}. For GM=6dB: |G|=10^(-6/20)=0.5 at ω_pc, find K. Then find ω_gc and PM.
